blockchain-cryptography.title: Blockchain & Cryptography Introduction
blockchain-cryptography.description: This course explores the fundamental concepts of Blockchain technology and Distributed Ledger Systems, with a heavy focus on the role of cryptography in security. You will study symmetric and asymmetric encryption, digital signatures, and hash functions while analyzing blockchain internals and consensus models like Proof of Work and Proof of Stake. The curriculum also examines real-world applications in cryptocurrencies, smart contracts, and Decentralized Finance (DeFi), alongside regulatory trends and emerging technologies.

assessment.details: 5 individual quiz tests — 1.2 points each, 6 points total

blockchain-cryptography.workflow: 30 hours of Lectures · 30 hours of Central Exercises.

database-1.title: Database 1
database-1.description: This course focuses on the systematic application of relational database concepts, ranging from conceptual design to physical implementation. You will master the ER model to conceptually model databases and learn to map those models into database schemes. The curriculum emphasizes practical application, requiring you to implement databases using SQL DDL, apply normalization rules, and ensure referential integrity. Additionally, you will gain a command of complex SQL querying — including CTEs and window functions — while learning to optimize query execution through an understanding of physical data organization and index structures.

assessment.details: Written exam covering database theory and SQL
Lecture contributions (7 pts) · Learning Journals (7 pts) · Lab tasks (7 pts)

database-1.workflow: 60 contact hours — 2 hours of lectures + 2 hours of labs per week. 120 hours of self-study.

theory-of-computation.title: Introduction to Theory of Computation
theory-of-computation.description: This course explores the fundamental limits of what computers can and cannot do. You will master four major theoretical areas: Formal Languages and machine models, Recursion Theory (computability), Formal Logic, and Complexity Theory. The curriculum is built around proving technical statements through diagonalization and simulation, covering essential concepts like Turing machines, Gödel's incompleteness theorems, and the P-versus-NP problem. You will learn to determine the computational difficulty of a problem and recognize when an algorithmic solution is mathematically impossible to construct.
theory-of-computation.prerequisites: Discrete Structures, Linear Algebra for Informatics, and Analysis for Informatics
theory-of-computation.workflow: 30 hours of Lectures · 15 hours of Central Exercises · 15 hours of Exercise classes. Students are expected to spend at least 1 hour of self-study for every hour of class.

scripting-languages.title: Scripting Languages
scripting-languages.description: This course focuses on practical front-end development, covering HTML fundamentals, CSS techniques (Flexbox, animations, and responsive design), and JavaScript (DOM manipulation, ES6+, and asynchronous programming). You will learn to build interactive web applications by creating templates, modules, and components using React. The curriculum is strictly project-based, requiring you to integrate these technologies into a team midterm and an individual final software package.
scripting-languages.workflow: 15 hours of Lectures · 15 hours of Central Exercises · 30 hours of Practical Labs.

advanced-java-in-practice.title: Advanced Java in Practice
advanced-java-in-practice.description: This course is designed for students seeking to deepen their understanding of Java through hands-on examples. You will explore advanced topics including multithreading and concurrency, functional programming with lambdas and streams, and the Java type system (generics, annotations, and reflection). The curriculum covers modern enhancements like records and sealed classes, alongside JVM internals, memory management, and practical applications in networking and database connectivity, all while emphasising real-world best practices.
advanced-java-in-practice.prerequisites: Introduction to Informatics and Fundamentals of Programming (Java)
advanced-java-in-practice.workflow: 30 hours of Lectures · 30 hours of Central Exercises · 30 hours of TTF.

applied-llm-systems.title: Applied LLM Systems
applied-llm-systems.description: This hands-on course focuses on building LLM applications through a framework-agnostic lens. You will master prompting, structured outputs, tool/function calling, and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). The curriculum covers advanced workflows like agents with memory, evaluation techniques, and production concerns including cost, latency, and safety. While primarily practical, the course provides the necessary theoretical foundation in pretraining, alignment (RLHF/DPO), and fine-tuning (LoRA/PEFT) to help you make informed architectural decisions.

assessment.minimum: Min. 21 / 40 to be eligible for finals

applied-llm-systems.workflow: 30 hours of Lectures · 30 hours of Lab sessions.

fundamentals-of-algorithms-and-data-stru.title: Fundamentals of Algorithms and Data Structures
fundamentals-of-algorithms-and-data-stru.posterLabel: Fundamentals of Algorithms and Data Structures course poster
fundamentals-of-algorithms-and-data-stru.description: This course bridges the gap between mathematical elegance and practical computational power, exploring the vital design patterns and data structures that drive efficient software. You will master foundational algorithmic paradigms—including divide-and-conquer, randomization, greedy strategies, and dynamic programming—while learning to rigorously evaluate resource consumption across worst-case, expected, and amortized settings. Moving beyond basic programming, you will unlock a powerful toolbox containing fast Fourier transforms, balanced trees, advanced graph concepts, and network algorithms. Through this mathematically grounded approach, you will discover how subtle, clever insights can drastically outpace straightforward solutions, transforming theoretical principles into high-performance computing realities.
fundamentals-of-algorithms-and-data-stru.prerequisites: Introduction to Informatics I, Discrete Structures

assessment.details: Solving 11–12 weekly or biweekly exercise worksheets and presenting solutions within exercise groups.
assessment.minimum: Combined with the Midterm Exam, students must earn at least 21 / 60 points.

assessment.details: A mid-semester written examination assessing early theoretical and practical components.
assessment.minimum: Combined with Exercises, students must earn at least 21 / 60 points.

assessment.details: A comprehensive final written examination covering the course content.
assessment.minimum: Min. 21 / 40 points to pass the exam.

fundamentals-of-algorithms-and-data-stru.workflow: 30 hours of Lectures · 15 hours of Central Exercises · 15 hours of Exercise classes. Students are expected to dedicate 120 hours to independent self-study, spending at least 1 hour digesting course materials and studying the textbook for every hour of class or central exercise.

databases-2.title: Databases 2
databases-2.description: This course explores the evolution of data management, charting the shift from traditional central relational structures to modern, multi-node distributed environments designed to tackle the challenges of "Big Data". Using the relational model as a foundational benchmark, you will dive deeply into database architectures, advanced storage mechanics (such as Log-Structured Merge-trees), and modern distributed models. You will rigorously dissect the mechanics of multiuser concurrency, diagnosing transaction anomalies, evaluating serializability, and analyzing isolation levels alongside standard protocols like locking and MVCC. Moving into distributed territory, the curriculum confronts the technical complexities of replication, sharding, and the critical trade-offs between strong and eventual consistency. Through hands-on environments featuring PostgreSQL, ScyllaDB, and MongoDB, you will learn to evaluate architectural trade-offs and make highly informed database design choices for real-world application use cases.
databases-2.prerequisites: Fundamentals of Databases 1, International B2 English Competence level or equivalent

assessment.details: Evaluated via contributing to lectures (7 points), compiling an Individual Learning Journal (6 points), and executing weekly lab implementation assignments (7 points).
assessment.minimum: Combined with the Midterm Exam, students must earn a minimum threshold of 25 / 60 points.

assessment.details: A written examination (paper, digital, or hybrid) administered during the university's predefined midterm week.
assessment.minimum: Combined with Active Participation, students must earn a minimum threshold of 25 / 60 points.

assessment.details: A comprehensive written examination organized after the 15th week, potentially requiring a functional database system running live on student laptops.
assessment.minimum: Min. 21 / 40 points to pass the exam.

databases-2.workflow: 60 Contact Hours, evenly distributed across lecture and lab sessions over a 15-week schedule, balanced alongside 120 hours of mandatory independent self-study, bringing the semester commitment to a total of 180 hours. Students are expected to systematically review lesson scripts, document personal learning journals weekly, and actively complete all laboratory assignments to internalize practical system errors and traps.

introduction-software-engineering.title: Introduction Software Engineering
introduction-software-engineering.description: This course transcends basic programming to explore the structural principles and systematic methodologies required to build, scale, and maintain medium-to-large software ecosystems. Moving through the complete software development lifecycle—from strategic project planning and algorithmic cost estimation to systemic analysis, design, implementation, and long-term maintenance—you will learn how to transform abstract human requirements into robust engineering blueprints. You will master the visual blueprints of the industry by constructing an expansive array of UML diagrams, using them to craft highly modular, object-oriented designs fortified by proven architectural styles and classic design patterns. Furthermore, the curriculum evaluates competing engineering lifecycles, contrasting traditional Waterfall and V-Models with contemporary Agile frameworks like Scrum and Extreme Programming, while establishing rigorous validation standards through controlflow-oriented testing methodologies.

assessment.details: 10 assignments (4 points each) designed to transition from theoretical understanding to true mastery. Late submissions incur a 10% penalty per day. Solutions are subject to strict anti-plagiarism and AI-generation policies (0 points for violations). Students are randomly selected by tutors to present and explain their work; failure to accurately explain a solution results in a 0 for the entire assignment.
assessment.minimum: Combined with the Midterm Exam, students must achieve a minimum competence threshold of 21 / 60 points.

assessment.details: A 2-hour, closed-book, closed-computer mid-semester examination covering early software lifecycle phases, concepts, and modeling techniques.
assessment.minimum: Combined with Homework (TTF), students must achieve a minimum competence threshold of 21 / 60 points.

assessment.details: A comprehensive, 2-hour, closed-book, closed-computer final written examination evaluating the entire breadth of the course material.
assessment.minimum: Min. 21 / 40 points to pass the exam.

introduction-software-engineering.workflow: 75 Contact Hours divided weekly into 3 hours of Lectures and 2 hours of Central Exercises (TTF) balanced alongside 115 hours of independent self-study, totaling a 190-hour semester commitment. Students are highly encouraged to attend all classes, eliminate digital distractions in the lecture hall, and systematically study slides and textbook chapters on the exact same day the material is presented.
